title: Please Excuse My Child: Unexcused Absences in Student Attendance and Achievement
author: Nat Malkus
text:
Key Points

Examining excused and unexcused absences separately can reveal important differences in students’ attendance behavior that are obscured by metrics like chronic absenteeism and absence rates, which treat all absences the same.

Using Indiana data, we find that unexcused absences follow distinct patterns from excused absences: Unexcused absences rise over the spring, while excused absences do not and rise more before and after weekends.

Even adjusting for other factors such as poverty and race, students with the top 20 percent highest absence rates are responsible for a disproportionate share of unexcused absences, meaning that chronic absenteeism is, to a large extent, unexcused absenteeism.

Unexcused absences reduce student test scores more than excused absences, suggesting that unexcused absences may be the prime target for efforts to improve attendance.

Executive Summary

Predominant measures of student attendance, such as attendance rates and chronic absenteeism, do not distinguish between excused and unexcused absences. Using statewide attendance records in Indiana public schools from the 2015–16 through 2023–24 school years, we examine excused and unexcused absences across students, schools, and time and how these differences relate to chronic absenteeism and academic achievement. We find that the two types of absence followed distinct patterns.

Unexcused absences were more common among students from historically disadvantaged groups. Even controlling for those factors, the absences of students with the top 20 percent highest absence rates were far more often unexcused, meaning that chronic absenteeism was disproportionately driven by unexcused absences.

The timing of unexcused and excused absences also differed: Unexcused absences rose over the spring, while excused absences did not, instead rising more before and after weekends. Adjusting for the time of year, an absence was more likely unexcused if a student had already accumulated more prior absences up to that point in the year.

Finally, unexcused absences reduced student test scores more than excused absences. This suggests that unexcused absences should be the prime target for attendance improvement efforts, though reducing unexcused absences may not directly improve students’ scores by these same amounts.

These findings show that systems for recording attendance meaningfully distinguish between the two types of absences, which may reflect different patterns of attendance behavior. Unexcused absences’ concentration among the most absent students, their spike around weekends, and their spring rise are consistent with their being driven by the choices of students and families, beyond illness or circumstance.

Introduction

During the COVID-19 pandemic, student absenteeism soared, and it remains high today.1 Part of what makes addressing absenteeism through policy so challenging is that there’s a simple question that’s hard to answer in detail and at scale—namely, why are students missing so much more school than before the pandemic? We and other researchers on the American Enterprise Institute’s Chronic Absenteeism Research Working Group have investigated this question from many angles: students’ self-reported reasons for absenteeism, daily and yearly patterns in absences, the demographics of chronic absenteeism, and more.2

How we measure attendance shapes what we notice about it. The primary accountability metric for attendance is chronic absenteeism, which simply indicates whether a student was absent at least 10 percent of the school year. This measure treats all absences as the same, and there’s good reason to treat them this way: Research has shown that every absence matters for student outcomes.3 At the same time, chronic absenteeism, or any simple absence rate, papers over important distinctions between kinds of absences.

Not all absences are created equal. Some absences are excused; others are unexcused. Pre-pandemic research showing that unexcused absences are a stronger drag on test scores suggests that the distinction is worth more attention.4 If schools’ attendance data systems are working (i.e., if schools accurately record excuses and categorize absences), then the excused and unexcused absence data reflect real differences in student or parent behavior.

However, there are reasons to doubt how well the attendance systems capture excused absences. “Unexcused” is the default absence record until a justification, like a sick note from a doctor or parent, is provided. In the case of truancy, no note or documentation should justify or excuse the absence, but that might not always be the case. These systems can fail, as illustrated in the blue boxes in Figure 1.

An illness with documentation would constitute a legitimate and excused absence (Cell 1). The same illness without a note—due to lack of access to a doctor or simply failing to provide one—would be a legitimate absence that a school would record as unexcused (Cell 2). In contrast, skipping school or taking a family vacation would be an illegitimate absence, and should be unexcused (Cell 4); however, a parent note can still convert unexcused to excused absences, even though they should not be converted. We have little way of knowing how prevalent, or rare, these exceptions are.

Acknowledging the potential uncertainty in attendance data, in this report we evaluate how patterns of excused and unexcused absences differ and how important the differences in those absences are. Specifically, we use a dataset of all students in Indiana to analyze when, where, and whose absences were excused and how unexcused absences relate to chronic absenteeism and student test scores.

Data

Our data consist of all student attendance records collected by the Indiana Department of Education, including demographics and aggregate attendance data, from the 2015–16 through 2023–24 academic years and day-level attendance records from the 2020–21 through 2023–24 academic years. (We hereafter refer to academic years by their latter calendar year—for example, we would use 2021 for the 2020–21 academic year.) The aggregated data include over 1.9 million students. In our analyses, we exclude students attending private and nonstandard schools, students attending prekindergarten, students who are otherwise outside the standard K–12 track, and students with missing demographic variables. (The longitudinal data, in Figure 5, are the exception.) Collectively, these excluded students make up roughly 11 percent of students in the Indiana dataset.

The data include counts of excused and unexcused absences by year from 2016 to 2020 and day-level records identifying absences as excused or unexcused from 2021 to 2024. What qualifies as an excused absence is not entirely clear. The state offers guidance to help Indiana school corporations (i.e., districts) decide which absences count as excused or unexcused, but ultimately, “school districts locally define ‘excused’ and ‘unexcused’ absences.”5

In practice, most districts likely follow conventions that are similar to each other and aligned with state guidance. Absences are typically excused for reasons such as illness (often verified by a note from a doctor or, in some cases, a parent or guardian), funeral attendance, maternity-related needs, or military-related family obligations. State law also requires schools to excuse absences for certain civic, legal, and military activities, as well as participation in the state fair. By default, absences are counted as unexcused unless a valid excuse—often with documentation—is provided.

Even though these definitions are certainly not applied uniformly across districts, we nonetheless assume that attendance practices are reasonably consistent within districts over time. Under that assumption, differences we observe—even in imperfect data—can offer useful insight.

Unexcused Absences Are Uneven Across Students

In 2024, about 41 percent of all absences in Indiana were unexcused, but only about one-third of the average student’s absences were unexcused.10 This difference is largely attributable to the rate of excused and unexcused absences varying dramatically across student demographics and school characteristics (Table A1).

It has long been established that the distribution of absences across students is skewed to the right, meaning that most students have relatively few absences but that relatively few students have many absences. We find that, in Indiana, this spike in absences for students on the right end of the distribution is overwhelmingly driven by unexcused absences. In fact, excused absences increase approximately linearly as you move up absence-rate deciles (Figure 2).

The same is true for unexcused absences, but only until you reach very high absence rates. In fact, students in the 10th absence-rate decile (the 10 percent of students who are most absent) have more than twice as many unexcused absences, on average, as students in the ninth decile, with unexcused-absence rates of 19 and 7 percent, respectively, in 2024. Strikingly, students in the 10th decile had, on average, more unexcused absences than students in the ninth decile had total absences.

A corollary of this spike in unexcused absences among highly absent students is that the excused share of absences was much lower for chronically absent students, especially students in the highest absence-rate decile (Figure 3). In 2024, for example, one-third of absences or fewer (27 to 33 percent) for students in the bottom eight absence-rate deciles were unexcused, but a majority of absences (55 percent) were unexcused for students in the top decile. Chronically absent students—a lower threshold for absences than the threshold for the 10th absence-rate decile—saw 52 percent of their absences unexcused, compared with 32 percent for other students.

Why does this excused-absence gap emerge between highly and moderately absent students? We see two possible explanations, both of which could be at work. The first is a difference in documentation. Unexcused-absence rates are higher among students from historically disadvantaged groups who might find it harder to have their absences marked as excused, even when the reasons for the absence are legitimate (Figure 5). For example, the unexcused shares of absences were higher among poor than nonpoor students (49 vs. 32 percent), black and Hispanic students compared with white students (67 and 52 percent vs. 31 percent), and students attending poorer schools (60 percent in the poorest quintile vs. 23 percent in the least poor quintile).

Poor and otherwise disadvantaged students make up a disproportionate number of the students in the top absence-rate deciles. These students might not only have more legitimate absences (e.g., due to illness or transportation difficulties); they might also find it harder to have their legitimate absences categorized as excused. If they find it harder to secure doctor’s notes or earn credence from those recording attendance, disadvantaged students’ unexcused-absence rates could be a documentation problem.

The second explanation is differences in behavior. All students face some unavoidable absences, for sickness or family emergencies. If the system classifies absences as intended, these should be excused. Frequently absent students have these baseline absences and then some. Part of that excess could be for reasons outside of students’ control, but it is difficult to believe that 10 percent of students, or even half as many, have unavoidable reasons to miss an average of 34 days of school (median 30 days) per school year, as we see in our data. More likely, a substantial share of these absences are choices. As absences accumulate, we expect that each additional absence is more likely to be for reasons schools deem illegitimate, such as skipping school or family vacations. If the system is working, excess absences should be unexcused more frequently than the baseline absences all students are at risk of. Having more absences means a larger share fall into this excess category, so on average, the unexcused rate should rise—as we observe.

The reality is that neither of these explanations—differences in classification or differences in behavior—is desirable. Either chronic absenteeism is driven by absences that schools have failed to deem legitimate, concentrated among already disadvantaged students, or it is driven by large numbers of actually illegitimate absences based on schools’ own attendance rules. Most likely, both explanations are at work in some combination.

Modeling Unexcused Absences Across Student Groups

To better understand what is driving unexcused absences, we built a series of statistical models to estimate the odds that a given absence was unexcused. (See the first sidebar and Table A2.) Indeed, our results are consistent with differences in both documentation and behavior potentially playing active roles. After adding controls, absences remain less likely to be excused among students with high absence rates, but the difference is less than half as large as in the raw data (Figure 6): According to our models, 42 percent of chronically absent students’ absences were unexcused in 2024, compared with 33 percent for non–chronically absent students. In relative terms, the odds (not probability) that an absence was unexcused were 65 percent higher for chronically absent students and 96 percent higher for students in the 10th absence-rate decile. These gaps are substantial, but they are much smaller than the raw differences in Figure 3.

Aside from whether a student was in the 10th absence-rate decile, we find higher odds of an unexcused absence by race (89 percent higher odds for black vs. white students), student poverty (39 percent higher odds for poor vs. nonpoor students), school poverty (147 percent higher for the poorest vs. the least poor quintile), grade (61 percent higher for 12th graders vs. third graders), achievement (84 percent higher for the top quintile vs. the lowest quintile), attending an urban school (50 percent higher for urban vs. rural), and gender (13 percent higher for male students vs. female students).12 Finally, as might be expected with more common medical issues, students in special education had 15 percent more total absences, on average; but controlling for other factors, their absences were less likely to be unexcused (12 percent lower odds, Table A2).

The effect of most variables in our models did not differ significantly for students with high versus moderate absenteeism. Exceptions include male students, whose higher likelihood than females for absences to be unexcused was even more pronounced among chronically absent students (Table A3). Meanwhile, compared with their wealthier peers, poor students’ absences were more likely to be unexcused, but this difference was smaller among chronically absent students.

Overall, these results in no way settle the question of which of the two explanations posited earlier is more correct—they don’t tell us why poor students, for instance, have higher rates of unexcused absences—but they do suggest both are at work. Some markers of disadvantage are associated with more unexcused absences. Even after controlling for many factors, students in the 10th decile for absence are still much more likely to have their absences counted as unexcused.

Unexcused Absences Vary Across Time

Unexcused absences appear to disproportionately drive chronic absenteeism. We examine patterns in excused and unexcused absences over time—in terms of years, months within the school year, and days in the week—to better understand what might drive them.

One theme in recent absenteeism research, including ours, is that the pandemic changed the quantity but not the quality of absences. That is, students are missing more days of school today than they did before the pandemic, but there has been remarkably little change in the patterns of their absences when it comes to the reasons they provide for their absences, when in the year or week those absences occur, the length of their absences, or how those absences cluster.13

This theme of consistency over years mostly holds when looking at excused and unexcused absences as well. Before the pandemic, excused absences outnumbered unexcused absences. But then in 2021, at the height of the pandemic and remote schooling (and when recordkeeping was questionable), the share of unexcused absences soared. In 2022, the pattern reversed and unexcused and excused absences plateaued at a new baseline, both higher than before the pandemic. Nevertheless, although the total number of absences is higher, the share of unexcused absences is largely similar—compared with 2019, about 4 percent higher in 2023 and 9 percent higher in 2024 (Figure 7). Not only is this rise very small, but it is also possible that it is not attributable to the pandemic—the share of unexcused absences was growing slowly before the pandemic, from 2016 to 2020 (with a small dip in 2017), and continued growing again after the pandemic, from 2023 to 2024.

Although the proportion of absences that are unexcused has not dramatically changed from before the pandemic, over the course of a given school year, excused and unexcused absences follow very different patterns. During the 2024 school year, both rates rose during the fall, but excused absences fell during the spring while unexcused absences plateaued or even rose slightly (Figure 6). As a result, unexcused absences rose from 35 percent of all absences in August 2023 to 55 percent of all absences in May 2024—a difference larger than the raw gap between poor and nonpoor students in 2024. (See Table A1.)

The monthly pattern of excused absences, in blue, matches the pattern of influenza-like illnesses (ILI) over the school year (Figure 8).14 The green line is from Indiana’s Influenza Dashboard syndromic data, which measure, by month in 2024, the average percentage of emergency room and urgent care visits in Indiana for patients age five through 24 classified as ILI.15 Both excused absences and the ILI rise in the fall, dip slightly in January, and then fall consistently after the February 2024 peak. In addition, the same pattern of excused absences is evident in every decile (Figure 9). Unexcused-absence rates follow a very different pattern from January on, suggesting that though legitimately excusable absences may be miscategorized as unexcused, these are likely a small share of the total.

This shift over the year toward unexcused absences is robust, with similar patterns across years and absence-rate quintiles. The question is why it occurs. We offer three possibilities. First, the drivers of absenteeism could change over the course of the year. The midyear excused-absence peak corresponds to ILI incidence, and unexcused absences may rise at the end of the year as spring weather might draw students and families outside for recreation and travel. Second, attendance recordkeeping could change over the course of the year. Late in the year, absences that would have been excused early in the year may nolonger be—for example, if teachers’ or attendance officers’ patience decreases as the year goes on or if front offices are less credulous after some students have already accumulated many absences. Third, students’ and parents’ initial level of engagement with school could diminish as the year wears on, and parents’ tolerance for missing school for unexcused reasons could increase, while engagement could reset with the fresh start of each academic year.

Another pattern that parallels the rise in unexcused absences over the year is the likelihood of absences to be unexcused as students accumulate them (Figure 10). For instance, a student’s 10th absence was more likely to be unexcused than their fifth, which in turn was more likely to be unexcused than their first. This ordering effect applies for students who accumulate different numbers of total absences—represented in different colors—meaning we can’t chalk the effect up to differences between students with higher and lower absence rates. We also see that the more total absences a student has over the course of the year, the more likely their first absence is unexcused. For example, among students with six to 11 absences in 2024, only 29 percent of their first absences were unexcused, rising thereafter, whereas among students with 18 or more absences, 41 percent of first absences were unexcused.

Is the rise in unexcused absences over the course of the year due to this ordering effect or just a general upward drift from fall to spring, as seen in Figure 6? To find out, we modeled the probability for a given absence to be unexcused based on the number of that student’s prior absences, controlling for the time of the year (Table A4). The probability that an absence was unexcused still rose with the number of prior absences. Though small, this effect is not trivial. In fact, controlling for other factors, we find the chance that the average student’s first absence was unexcused was 36 percent, compared with a 42 percent chance for their 10th absence.

The ordering effect accounted for all the seasonal rise in absences early in the school year but not later in the year. Put differently, if we remove the ordering effect and treat every absence as if it were the student’s first, the probability that a given absence is excused remains nearly constant from August through January (dotted line), but it rises in the final months of the school year (Figure 11).

Finally, we look at excused- and unexcused-absence patterns within school weeks. Specifically, we build on prior work showing that total absences were more common on the first day of the week (typically Monday—or Tuesday on short weeks) and the last day of the week (typically Friday) than in the middle of the week.16 If unexcused absences drive these weekend-adjacent absence spikes without legitimate explanations, it would suggest that unexcused absences are more often driven by family and student choices.

The total absence rate in 2024 was 10 percent higher on the first day than in the middle of the week and 22 percent higher on the last day of the week (Figure 12). Unexcused absences varied more—about 12 percent higher on the first day and 36 percent higher on the last day of the week. In contrast, excused absences showed less variation compared with the middle of the week, higher by 9 percent on the first and 13 percent on the last day of the week.17 Interestingly, these differences are evident among not only the most absent students but students at every decile of total absences. While it’s possible that sickness or other unmeasured factors could vary over days of the school week, the most obvious explanation is that students and families more often choose to take off the week’s last school day to extend their weekends.

Excused and Unexcused Absences and Test Scores

Thus far, the data show that unexcused absences disproportionately drive chronic absenteeism, have different patterns than excused absences, and occur at times more consistent with family and student choices than excused absences occur. These findings also suggest that attendance records reflect real differences across excused and unexcused absences. To the extent this is so, it begs a final question: Do excused and unexcused absences have different effects on student outcomes?

Figure 13 shows the reduction in math and ELA test scores associated with 10 excused or unexcused absences in grades four to eight, measured in standard deviations, after controlling for prior-year achievement and other factors (Table A5). Consistent with pre-pandemic research in US schools, unexcused absences are associated with larger declines than excused absences across both subjects.18 Declines for both excused and unexcused absences in ELA were smaller than those in math, but the gaps between absence types were larger (by 2.1 percentage points of a standard deviation in math compared with3.0 points in ELA). For these grades in 2024, 38 percent of absences in grades four to eight were unexcused, but with larger effects: Unexcused absences were associated with 49 percent of the total absence-related test-score declines in math and 67 percent of the declines in ELA. When it comes to test scores, not all absences are created equal.

Conclusion

Our examination of Indiana attendance data suggests that unexcused absences may matter more than is reflected in much of the research and discourse on post-pandemic absenteeism. Three main results emerge from our analyses.

First, unexcused absences are meaningfully different from excused absences. As the primary attendance metric, chronic absenteeism can obscure these differences. So does the real possibility that the legitimacy of absences is not always accurately reflected in school absence records. However, at least in Indiana, the distinctions in excused- and unexcused-absence rates by student characteristics, students’ annual attendance rates, and the timing over the course of the school year and school week demonstrate that the two categories are indeed distinct. Of course, some excused and unexcused absences are miscategorized in the data based on their genuine legitimacy, as illustrated in Figure 1. But that only highlights the importance of the distinction between the categories. If we see large measurable differences with noisy data, these findings are a lower bound on the true differences between absences that occur for excusable and inexcusable reasons.

Second, unexcused absences make a big difference, especially for the most absent students. In contrast to excused absences, which increase more subtly and smoothly across absence deciles, unexcused absences are concentrated among the most absent students and are a major driver of chronic absenteeism. Unexcused absences of students in the top 20 percent of annual absences also reshape the curve of absences, and make the swoosh shape, across grades. Though partially explained by other important factors—like student poverty, race, and school poverty—the spike in unexcused absences differentiates the students with the lowest attendance rates.

Third, unexcused absences matter for student test scores, more so than excused absences. These differences are stark and suggest unexcused absences should be the prime target of efforts to improve attendance. Importantly, while the disadvantages mentioned above explain part of the elevated unexcused absences among the most absent students, these negative effects on students’ test scores accrue to much larger losses for these disadvantaged students. Some caution is warranted here, as we cannot infer that reducing unexcused absences will directly improve students’ scores by these same amounts, since student engagement is likely related to both unexcused absences and test scores. It’s also worth stating that these results should not be taken to support any effort to convert unexcused absences into excused absences; rather, the appropriate goal is to convert unexcused absences into attendance.

These results fit into a broader research program investigating the causes, consequences, and nature of post-pandemic absenteeism. At least in Indiana, the share of absences that are excused has not changed meaningfully since before the pandemic. This stability matches patterns emerging from prior research showing that the pandemic changed the quantity, but not the quality, of student absences. Though elevated, the weekly rhythms, seasonal patterns, and distribution of absences and their effects on student test scores look much as they did before COVID.

At the same time, our results raise difficult questions about agency that the chronic absenteeism literature has largely sidestepped. We cannot conclude from these results whether agency, on the part of parents or students, is the central driver of post-pandemic absenteeism, but the concentration of unexcused absences among the most absent students, the Friday spike, and the spring drift all point toward choices that go beyond illness or circumstance. The large raw differences in unexcused absences across demographic groups do shrink when controls are added for poverty and race, but they nonetheless amount to substantially more missed school, and missed learning, for these students. Given the challenge post-pandemic chronic absenteeism still poses to schools and students, a clear-eyed assessment of unexcused absences’ role is an essential part of a functional response.

About the Authors 

Nat Malkus is a senior fellow and the deputy director of education policy at the American Enterprise Institute (AEI) and an affiliate of AEI’s James Q. Wilson Program in K–12 Education Studies. He is the creator of AEI’s Return to Learn Tracker, which tracked pandemic school closures and masking policies and currently tracks post-pandemic chronic absenteeism.

Sam Hollon is a data analyst in education policy studies at the American Enterprise Institute.

The Nike swoosh is not a perfect match for this absence-rate pattern, because the end points of the swoosh are actually of equal height, while high school absence rates are well above those in kindergarten. But the swoosh shape is useful because to many people, it feels higher on the right side and helps them grasp the pattern intuitively with the illustration. Figure 4 displays data from 2024, but other post-pandemic years show very similar patterns.
The student-level estimates come from Model 3. The school poverty and urbanicity estimates come from Model 1, because Model 1 replaces school-level controls with school fixed effects. The only large difference was race: The black odds ratio was 2.14 in Model 1, compared with 1.89 in Model 3. See Table A2 for the full estimates from all models.
